1.hint: Count the straight edges around a triangle — how many sides meet at the corners?
1.help:
A **triangle** is a flat shape with three straight sides. Each side is a line segment between two corners.

Example: a triangle drawn on paper has exactly **3** sides, no matter how tall or wide it looks.

2.hint: A square has four equal sides — how many sides is that in total?
2.help:
A **square** is a quadrilateral with four equal sides and four equal angles.

Example: a chessboard square has **4** sides.

3.hint: Corners are the points where two sides meet — count them on a rectangle.
3.help:
A **rectangle** has four sides, and each corner is where two sides meet. That gives **4** corners (vertices).

Example: a door frame (roughly rectangular) has four corners.

4.hint: Which common shape is completely round with no straight edges?
4.help:
Shapes with **no straight sides** are curved all the way round. A **circle** (or a ball’s outline) fits this.

Example: a wheel or a coin face is circular — no straight sides.

5.hint: Compare how many sides each named shape has: circle, triangle, or square.
5.help:
Count sides: a circle has **none** (only a curve), a triangle has **3**, a square has **4**.

Example: only the **triangle** has exactly three straight sides.

6.hint: Which shape must have all four sides the same length: square or rectangle?
6.help:
A **square** always has four **equal** sides. A rectangle has four sides but only opposite pairs are equal — the sides can be different lengths.

Example: a 5 cm by 8 cm rectangle is **not** a square.

7.hint: Does a circle have sharp corners where two straight lines meet?
7.help:
A circle is one smooth curve. It has **no corners** — no vertices where straight sides meet.

So the statement is **false**.

8.hint: “Penta-” often means five — how many sides does a pentagon have?
8.help:
A **pentagon** is a polygon with **5** straight sides. Think of a five-sided road sign shape.

Example: a regular pentagon has 5 equal sides and 5 corners.

9.hint: Lines that stay the same distance apart and never cross — what are they called?
9.help:
**Parallel** lines lie in the same direction and never meet, like railway tracks in the distance.

Example: opposite edges of a ruler are parallel.

10.hint: When two lines meet at 90°, they are at right angles — what word describes that?
10.help:
**Perpendicular** lines cross at a **right angle** (90°). You often mark the corner with a small square.

Example: the corner of a sheet of paper is perpendicular sides.

11.hint: Sides and vertices (corners) — count both for a triangle.
11.help:
Every side of a triangle joins two **vertices**. A triangle has **3** sides and **3** vertices.

Example: an equilateral triangle still has 3 sides and 3 corners.

12.hint: A quadrilateral has exactly four sides — which listed shapes fit that?
12.help:
Pick shapes with **4** straight sides: **square**, **rectangle**, and **rhombus** are quadrilaterals. A triangle has 3 sides; a pentagon has 5.

Example: a kite with four sides is also a quadrilateral.

13.hint: Four equal angles in a square — each one is a right angle. How many degrees is that?
13.help:
A **right angle** is **90°**. A square has four equal angles, so each angle is **90°**.

Example: the corner of a book is 90°.

14.hint: All three sides different lengths — which triangle name fits?
14.help:
**Scalene** means no sides are equal. **Isosceles** has two equal sides; **equilateral** has three equal sides.

Example: sides 3 cm, 4 cm, 5 cm → **scalene**.

15.hint: Exactly two sides the same length — pick the matching triangle type.
15.help:
**Isosceles** triangles have **two equal sides** (and often two equal angles opposite those sides).

Example: sides 5 cm, 5 cm, 7 cm → isosceles.

16.hint: Can every rectangle be a square, or only some rectangles?
16.help:
A rectangle needs four right angles, but sides can be different lengths. A **square** is a special rectangle where **all four sides are equal**.

So **not every** rectangle is a square — only those with equal sides.

17.hint: All sides equal and all angles equal — which triangle name is that?
17.help:
**Equilateral** means **equal sides**. All three angles are also equal (60° each).

Example: a triangle with sides 4 cm, 4 cm, 4 cm is equilateral.

18.hint: On a pentagon, count sides and matching corners (vertices).
18.help:
A pentagon has **5** sides and **5** vertices — each side connects two corners.

Example: a regular pentagon has 5 equal sides and 5 corners.

19.hint: “Hex-” suggests six — how many sides on a hexagon?
19.help:
A **hexagon** has **6** straight sides. Think of a honeycomb cell outline.

Example: a regular hexagon has six equal sides.

20.hint: Four-sided flat shape — the special maths name starts with “quad…”.
20.help:
“Quad” means four. A **quadrilateral** is any four-sided polygon.

Example: squares and rectangles are quadrilaterals.

21.hint: Four right angles — must it always be a rectangle, or can another shape fit?
21.help:
A rectangle is defined as a quadrilateral with four right angles. Side lengths do not have to be different.

A square also has four right angles, so it belongs to the rectangle family as well as the square family.

22.hint: Use one word for lines that never meet and another for lines that meet at 90°.
22.help:
**Parallel** lines remain the same distance apart and never meet. **Perpendicular** lines meet at a right angle.

For example, opposite edges of a rectangular page are parallel, while two edges that meet at a corner are perpendicular.

23.hint: Two pairs of equal sides — could the shape be a rhombus or kite instead of a rectangle?
23.help:
A **rectangle** needs four **right angles**. A **rhombus** can have two pairs of equal sides but slanted angles — not necessarily a rectangle.

Example: a diamond shape (rhombus) has equal sides but is not a rectangle unless angles are 90°.

24.hint: A square has four right angles and four equal sides — is that allowed for a rectangle?
24.help:
A **rectangle** has four right angles. A **square** has four right angles **and** four equal sides — so a square **is** a special type of rectangle.

Sam is **correct** if rectangles are defined with right angles only.

25.hint: Can a triangle have zero equal sides, or always at least two equal?
25.help:
Test the statement against different triangle families. An isosceles triangle has two equal sides, while a scalene triangle has none.

Because the statement works for some triangles but not all of them, use the matching always-sometimes-never category.

26.hint: Rhombus and square both have four equal sides — compare their corner angles.
26.help:
Both have **four equal sides**. A **square** has four **90°** angles. A **rhombus** has equal sides but angles are not all 90° unless it is a square.

Example: a tilted diamond is a rhombus but not a square.

27.hint: Regular means equal sides AND equal angles — can angles differ if sides match?
27.help:
A **regular polygon** has **all sides equal and all angles equal**. You cannot have equal sides but different angle sizes and still call it regular.

The statement is **false**.

28.hint: Opposite sides parallel, four sides, not all equal — think of a slanted box shape.
28.help:
Four sides with **opposite sides parallel** is a **parallelogram**. If not all sides are equal, it is not a rhombus or square.

Example: a pushed-over rectangle is a parallelogram.

29.hint: Mark a 90° corner with a small square — perpendicular means meeting at right angles.
29.help:
Sketch two lines meeting at **90°**. Mark the angle with a little square in the corner.

**Perpendicular** means the lines cross at a right angle — like the corner of a room.

30.hint: Count sides and corners on a hexagon — compare to a square’s four sides.
30.help:
A regular **hexagon** has **6** sides and **6** vertices. A **square** has **4** of each.

Example: hexagon tiles often tessellate; squares have fewer sides.

31.hint: Octagon: “oct” suggests eight — count sides and vertices.
31.help:
An **octagon** has **8** sides and **8** vertices. A stop sign is a regular octagon.

Example: 8 equal sides on a regular octagon.

32.hint: Think of the geometry word for two sides next to each other.
32.help:
**Adjacent sides** share a vertex, so they meet at the same corner. **Opposite sides** face each other and do not share a corner.

For example, the top and right edges of a square are adjacent.

33.hint: A diagonal splits a rectangle into two triangles — are they the same shape and size?
33.help:
A rectangle diagonal makes two **right-angled triangles**. They are **congruent** (same size and shape) because they share the diagonal and matching sides.

Example: 6 m by 4 m rectangle → two identical right triangles.

34.hint: Equilateral triangle — what do you know about all three sides and all three angles?
34.help:
**Equilateral**: all **three sides equal**. All **three angles equal** — each is **60°**.

Example: road sign triangle often equilateral — equal sides and equal angles.

35.hint: Angles on a straight line add to 180° — subtract the angle you know from 180°.
35.help:
A straight line is **180°**. If one angle is 65°, the other is $180 - 65 = 115°$.

Example: on a line, 40° and 140° add to 180°.

36.hint: Angles around a point add to 360° — add the two given angles, then subtract from 360°.
36.help:
Full turn around a point is **360°**. $90 + 100 = 190$, so the third angle is $360 - 190 = 170°$.

Example: 120°, 120°, 120° around a point sum to 360°.

37.hint: Triangle angles sum to 180° — two angles are 70°, so what is left?
37.help:
In any triangle, angles add to **180°**. Two angles of 70° use $70 + 70 = 140°$, so the third is $180 - 140 = 40°$.

Example: isosceles with 80°, 80°, 20°.

38.hint: One full turn is how many degrees when you spin all the way round?
38.help:
A complete rotation is **360°** — like turning to face the same direction again.

Example: four right angles (90° each) make 360°.

39.hint: Add 40° and 60°, then subtract from 180° for the missing triangle angle.
39.help:
Triangle angle sum: $40 + 60 = 100°$. Missing angle: $180 - 100 = 80°$.

Example: 30°, 60°, 90° triangle sums to 180°.

40.hint: Reflex angle is the big angle outside the acute one — they add to 360° around a point.
40.help:
Angles around a point total **360°**. Acute 35° leaves reflex $360 - 35 = 325°$.

Example: acute 20° → reflex 340°.

41.hint: Recall the UK name for a quadrilateral with one parallel pair.
41.help:
Classify quadrilaterals by their parallel sides. A parallelogram has two parallel pairs, while a trapezium has one parallel pair.

A sloping tabletop outline can be a useful example of the second shape.

42.hint: Compare right-angled triangles with acute triangles.
42.help:
A right-angled triangle has one 90° angle. An acute triangle has three angles below 90°, so it has no right angle.

When a statement is true for one kind of triangle but false for another, it is **sometimes** true.

43.hint: Polygon names depend on the number of sides, not whether the sides are equal.
43.help:
A polygon with six straight sides belongs to the hexagon family. If its sides or angles are not all equal, it is irregular.

For example, changing one side length of a regular five-sided shape still leaves a pentagon.

44.hint: Square and rectangle always have 90° corners — what about rhombus and kite?
44.help:
**Square** and **rectangle**: always at least one right angle (actually four).

**Rhombus**: not always — only when it is a square.

**Kite**: not always — angles can all be non-right.

Sort using whether a right angle is **guaranteed**.

45.hint: Vertices are corners — on a regular hexagon, count corners, not side length.
45.help:
A **hexagon** has **6** vertices (corners), one at each join of two sides.

Example: a honeycomb cell outline has six corners.

46.hint: A square has four equal sides — does that match the definition of a rhombus?
46.help:
A **rhombus** has **four equal sides**. A **square** also has four equal sides (and four right angles), so every square **is** a rhombus.

Answer: **true**.

47.hint: “Hept-” means seven — write the seven-sided polygon name.
47.help:
A **heptagon** has **7** straight sides.

Example: some coins are heptagonal.

48.hint: Quadrilaterals have four sides — which listed shape has only three?
48.help:
A **triangle** has **3** sides, so it is **not** a quadrilateral. Kite, trapezium, and parallelogram all have four sides.

Pick **triangle**.

49.hint: Fold a square in half both ways and diagonally — how many mirror lines?
49.help:
A **square** has **4** lines of symmetry: vertical, horizontal, and two diagonals.

Example: a square tile looks the same in four mirror positions.

50.hint: Corner to corner across the middle of a rectangle — what is that segment called?
50.help:
A **diagonal** joins two vertices that are **not** next to each other.

Example: from top-left to bottom-right of a rectangle.

51.hint: UK name for a four-sided shape with exactly one pair of parallel sides.
51.help:
A **trapezium** has **one pair** of parallel sides. (US: trapezoid.)

Example: a slanted table top shape.

52.hint: Rhombus definition — are all four sides always the same length?
52.help:
By definition, a **rhombus** has **four equal sides**.

So the statement is **true** (angles may still slant).

53.hint: All three angles under 90° — which triangle name fits?
53.help:
An **acute-angled** triangle has **all angles less than 90°**.

Example: 60°, 60°, 60° is acute-angled.

54.hint: Matching shapes exactly — the word starts with “con…”.
54.help:
**Congruent** shapes are the **same size and shape** — one fits exactly on the other.

Example: two identical tiles are congruent.

55.help:
A **nonagon** has **9** straight sides.

Example: some coins are nine-sided.

56.hint: Is 88° smaller than a right angle (90°)?
56.help: **Acute** angles are **less than 90°**. Since $88 < 90$, the angle is **acute**.

57.hint: Is 91° just past a right angle?
57.help: **Obtuse** angles are **greater than 90°** but less than 180°. $91°$ is **obtuse**.

58.hint: A half turn on a straight line — what angle name fits 180°?
58.help:
A **straight angle** is exactly **180°** — a flat line.

Example: angles on a straight line add to 180°.

59.hint: Same number of vertices as sides on a pentagon — how many?
59.help:
A **pentagon** has **5** vertices (corners), one at each end of the five sides.

Example: a regular pentagon has five corners.

60.hint: Opposite sides parallel in both directions — name the quadrilateral family.
60.help:
A **parallelogram** has **both pairs** of opposite sides **parallel**.

Example: a pushed-over rectangle is a parallelogram.

61.hint: Four equal sides but not always four right angles — rectangle or rhombus?
61.help:
A **rhombus** always has **four equal sides**. A rectangle need not have all sides equal.

Pick **rhombus**.

62.hint: Equilateral means three equal sides — does that include two equal sides?
62.help:
**Isosceles** means at least **two equal sides**. **Equilateral** has **three** equal sides, so it **is** also isosceles.

Answer: **true**.

63.help:
A **decagon** has **10** straight sides.

Example: some medals are decagonal.

64.hint: Draw both diagonals inside a rectangle — how many different diagonals?
64.help:
A rectangle has **2** diagonals: they cross in the middle and join opposite corners.

Example: from corner A to C and from B to D.

65.hint: Polygons use straight sides only — does a circle qualify?
65.help:
A **polygon** is made of **straight** segments. A **circle** is one **curve**, so it is **not** a polygon.

Answer: **no**.

66.hint: Crossing at 90° — same word as in question 10.
66.help:
**Perpendicular** lines meet at a **right angle (90°)**.

Example: the corner of a book.

67.hint: No equal sides at all — which triangle type?
67.help:
**Scalene** triangles have **no equal sides**.

Example: sides 3 cm, 4 cm, 5 cm.

68.hint: A non-square rectangle: mirror through the middle horizontally and vertically only.
68.help:
A rectangle that is **not a square** has **2** lines of symmetry (horizontal and vertical through the centre).

A square would have four.

69.hint: Kite definition — two pairs of equal sides next to each other.
69.help:
A **kite** has **two pairs of equal adjacent sides** by definition.

Answer: **true**.

70.hint: More than one vertex — what is the plural word?
70.help:
One **vertex**, many **vertices** (corners where sides meet).

Example: a triangle has three vertices.

71.hint: Seven sides vs eight sides — which name has more?
71.help: An **octagon** has **8** sides; a **heptagon** has **7**. So **octagon** has more sides.

72.hint: Angles between 180° and 360° — what type is 270°?
72.help:
A **reflex** angle is **greater than 180°** and less than 360°. **270°** is reflex.

Example: the outside of a sharp corner.

73.hint: Triangle angles sum to 180° — add 55 and 60, then subtract from 180.

74.hint: Parallelogram opposite sides — same length as well as parallel.
74.help:
In a **parallelogram**, opposite sides are **parallel** and **equal in length**.

Missing word: **equal**.

75.hint: Rectangle has four right angles — is that enough to be a parallelogram?
75.help:
Every **rectangle** has opposite sides parallel, so it **is** a parallelogram.

Answer: **true**.

76.help:
A **quadrilateral** has **4** vertices (corners).

Example: squares, kites, and trapeziums all have four vertices.

77.hint: Circles are not made of straight segments — they are not a ___.
77.help:
Only shapes with straight sides are **polygons**. A circle is **not** a polygon.

Missing word: **polygon**.

78.hint: Exactly a right angle — pick 89°, 90°, or 92°.
78.help:
A **right angle** is exactly **90°**.

So choose **90°**.

Example: the corner of a sheet of paper.

79.hint: Four equal sides, angles not all 90° — name the quadrilateral.
79.help:
A **rhombus** has four equal sides but need not have right angles.

Example: a tilted diamond shape.

80.hint: Regular pentagon — are all five angles the same size?
80.help:
A **regular pentagon** has **five equal angles** (each $108°$) as well as equal sides.

Answer: **true**.

81.hint: Between 90° and 180° — name the angle type.
81.help:
An **obtuse** angle is **more than 90°** and **less than 180°**.

Example: 120° is obtuse.

82.help:
A **decagon** has **10** sides.

Example: some coins are ten-sided.

83.hint: Slanted parallelogram without right angles — still a rectangle?
83.help:
A **parallelogram** is a **rectangle** only when **all angles are 90°**.

Otherwise it is **sometimes** a rectangle — only in the right-angle case.

84.hint: Honeycomb cells — six sides or five?
84.help:
Honeycomb cells are usually **hexagons** (**6** sides).

Example: beeswax cells tessellate with hexagons.

85.help:
A triangle with a **90°** angle is **right-angled**.

Example: 3-4-5 triangles are right-angled.

86.hint: Scalene means unequal sides — can the largest angle still be 90°?
86.help:
A **scalene** triangle can still be **right-angled** (e.g. sides 3, 4, 5 cm).

Answer: **true**.

87.hint: Isosceles — at least how many sides match?
87.help:
**Isosceles** means **at least two equal sides** (often exactly two).

Minimum count: **2** equal sides.

88.help:
A mirror line is a **line of symmetry**.

Example: fold a rectangle in half — the crease is a line of symmetry.

89.hint: 179° is just under a straight line but well past 90°.
89.help: **Obtuse** angles are between **90° and 180°**. **179°** is **obtuse** (not reflex).

91.hint: Square diagonals cross in the middle — do they make 90° there?

92.help:
A **pentagon** has **5** sides.

Example: a home badge shaped like a pentagon.

93.hint: Which capital letter looks like a slanted diamond (rhombus) in block font?
93.help:
Among **O**, **H**, and **D**, capital **D** is often drawn with a **slanted** outer edge in block lettering, closest to a **rhombus** outline in some styles.

Choose **D**.

94.hint: Octagon vertices equal octagon sides — how many?
94.help:
An **octagon** has **8** vertices.

Example: a stop sign has eight corners.

95.help:
A **trapezium** must have **at least one pair of parallel sides**.

Answer: **true**.

96.hint: Equal sides and equal angles together — regular or irregular?
96.help:
A **regular** polygon has **all sides equal and all angles equal**.

Missing word: **regular**.

97.hint: Isosceles with 40° at the top — base angles are equal.
97.help: Angles sum to **180°**. Top **40°** leaves $140°$ for the two equal base angles: $140 ÷ 2 = 70°$ each.

98.hint: Four equal sides and four 90° angles — name it.
98.help: A **square** has **four equal sides** and **four right angles**.

99.hint: Rectangle diagonals — same length in any rectangle?
99.help:
Both diagonals of a **rectangle** are **equal in length**.

Answer: **true**.

100.hint: All three angles equal — triangle name?
100.help: If all **three angles are equal**, each is **60°** — an **equilateral** triangle.

101.hint: Rhombus with slanted angles — when does it become a square?
101.help:
A **rhombus** is a **square** only when **all angles are 90°**.

So **sometimes** a rhombus is a square.

102.hint: Polygons use straight edges — what does a circle use instead?
102.help:
Explain that **polygons** have **straight sides**, but a **circle** is a **curve** with no straight segments.

One sentence is enough.

103.hint: Four equal sides could be a rhombus with non-90° angles.
103.help:
A **rhombus** has four equal sides but is **not always** a square.

Answer: **false**.

104.hint: Regular means equal sides AND equal angles.
104.help:
If angles are **not all equal**, the hexagon is **not regular** even if sides match.

Answer: **no**.

105.hint: Kite equal sides touch — opposite or adjacent?
105.help:
Kites have **adjacent** equal sides, **not opposite** pairs like a rectangle.

Answer: **never** for opposite pairs.

106.hint: Compare corner angles: rectangle vs general parallelogram.
106.help: Say that a **rectangle** has **four right angles**, while a **parallelogram** only needs **opposite angles equal**.

107.hint: Isosceles with two equal sides — need all three equal?
107.help:
Give a counter-example: **5, 5, 8** is isosceles but **not** equilateral.

Answer: **false**.

108.hint: Count pairs of parallel sides on each shape.

109.hint: Three straight sides — always what family?
109.help:
Any closed shape with **three sides** is a **triangle**.

Answer: **always**.

110.help:
Mia is **correct**: every **quadrilateral** has exactly **four vertices** (corners).

Example: a kite has four corners.

112.hint: Need a four-sided shape that is not a parallelogram.
112.help: Examples: **trapezium** (only one pair parallel) or some **kites**.

113.hint: Parallelogram opposite angles — equal or different?
113.help:
Opposite angles in a **parallelogram** are **equal**.

Answer: **always**.

114.hint: Square fits rectangle rules and rhombus rules.
114.help: Mention **four right angles** (rectangle) and **four equal sides** (rhombus).

115.hint: Can more than one angle in a triangle be over 90°?
115.help:
Only **one** angle can be obtuse — the other two must be acute.

Answer: **true**.

116.hint: Square corner angles are 90° — what triangles appear when you cut along a diagonal?
116.help: Each half is a **right-angled isosceles** triangle (45°, 45°, 90° in a square cut).

117.hint: Regular polygon definition — sides match and angles match.
117.help:
**Regular** ⇒ **equal sides** (and equal angles).

Answer: **always** for the word regular.

118.hint: Pentagon only means five sides — equal sides optional.
118.help: Dev is **wrong**: any five-sided shape is a pentagon; sides need not be equal unless **regular**.

119.hint: Parallel lines go on forever — can one be shorter?
119.help:
Parallel lines can be **different lengths** (think of two lines on a page).

Answer: **false**.

121.hint: Try a right-angled triangle — how many acute angles left?
121.help: Even with one **90°** angle, the other **two** are acute — so **always** at least two acute angles.

122.hint: Next-door sides vs sides across the shape.
122.help: **Adjacent** sides share a vertex. **Opposite** sides do not touch at a corner.

123.hint: Can a hexagon push inward on one corner?
123.help:
A **concave** hexagon can have one interior angle **greater than 180°**.

Answer: **true**.

124.hint: Count mirror lines for square vs long rectangle.

125.help:
Four equal sides ⇒ **rhombus**.

Answer: **always**.

Example: a diamond playing-card shape is a rhombus.

126.hint: Rectangle needs four right angles, not one.
126.help: One right angle is **not enough** — answer **no** and say you need **four** right angles.

127.hint: Equilateral triangle — fold through each vertex.
127.help:
An **equilateral** triangle has **3** lines of symmetry.

Answer: **true**.

128.hint: Regular vs irregular hexagon — equal sides/angles or not.
128.help: **Regular**: all sides **and** angles equal. **Irregular**: at least one side or angle differs.

129.help:
**Vertex** and **corner** mean the same point where sides meet.

Answer: **always**.

130.hint: Equilateral road sign — all angles equal in any triangle sum to 180°.
130.help:
Three equal angles in a triangle sum to **180°**, so each is $180 ÷ 3 = 60°$.

Example: an equilateral road sign.

131.hint: General parallelogram — mirror line may be missing.
131.help:
Only special parallelograms (rectangle/rhombus/square) always have symmetry lines.

Answer: **sometimes**.

133.hint: Two pairs of parallel sides — parallelogram test.
133.help:
That description is exactly a **parallelogram**.

Answer: **always**.

134.hint: Different length and width — square or not?
134.help: Use **different side lengths** to show a rectangle is **not always** a square.

135.hint: Obtuse triangle — only one angle over 90°.
135.help:
The other two angles are **acute** — not all obtuse.

Answer: **false**.

136.hint: Regular octagon — how many equal angles at corners?
136.help: A regular **octagon** has **8** equal interior angles (one at each vertex).

137.hint: Compare diagonal length to a short side in a rectangle.
137.help:
A diagonal is the **longest** segment across the shape — **never** shorter than every side.

Answer: **never**.

138.hint: Adjacent equal sides, no parallel sides — kite or rhombus?
138.help: A **kite** fits: equal **adjacent** pairs, not necessarily parallel sides.

139.hint: Two perpendicular lines make four 90° angles at the cross.
139.help:
When lines are **perpendicular**, all **four** angles at the intersection are **90°**.

Answer: **true**.

140.hint: Rhombus: four equal sides. Kite: two pairs of adjacent equal sides.

141.hint: Minimum sides for a polygon — triangles count.
141.help:
A polygon needs **at least three** sides.

Answer: **always**.

142.hint: Quadrilateral means four — they counted five sides.
142.help: They mis-named it: **five sides** means **pentagon**, not quadrilateral.

143.hint: Acute angles are greater than 0° and less than 90°.
143.help:
**0°** is not considered **acute** in GCSE style.

Answer: **false**.

144.hint: Equilateral triangle — three equal angles in 180°.
144.help:
Each angle in an **equilateral** triangle is **60°** because $180 ÷ 3 = 60$.

Example: equal angles on a triangular sign.

145.hint: Square has two pairs of parallel sides.

146.hint: Opposite sides parallel — same direction, never meet.
146.help: Describe **parallel** opposite sides staying the same distance apart.

147.help:
**Heptagon** has **more** sides than a hexagon.

Statement is **false**.

148.hint: One pair parallel, other pair not — UK name.
148.help:
That is a **trapezium** — one pair of **parallel** sides and one pair that is not.

Example: a slanted table top.

149.hint: Two 90° angles already use 180° — room for a third angle?
149.help:
Two right angles sum to **180°** — impossible in a triangle.

Answer: **never**.

150.hint: Flat face of a cylinder — which 2D shape?
150.help:
The flat end of a cylinder is a **circle** — one curved edge, no straight sides.

Example: a tin can viewed from above.

151.hint: Regular pentagon badge — sides and corners match.
151.help:
A pentagon has **5 sides** and **5 vertices** — one corner at each join of sides.

Example: a five-sided badge.

152.hint: Chessboard square — name and count right angles.
152.help:
It is a **square** with **four** right angles (90° at each corner).

Example: a chessboard square.

153.hint: Equal sloping sides on each half of the chevron.
153.help:
Equal sloping sides on each half ⇒ **isosceles** triangles.

Example: a chevron arrow made from two matching triangles.

154.help:
Only a **square** has perpendicular diagonals among rectangles.

Answer: **no**.

155.hint: Honeycomb tiling — how many hexagons meet at one point?
155.help: In a flat honeycomb pattern, **3** hexagons meet at each vertex.

156.hint: Stop-sign shape — count sides and vertices.
156.help:
An **octagon** has **8 sides** and **8 vertices**.

Example: a stop sign outline.

157.help:
A **rhombus** needs **all four sides equal**. This kite is **not** a rhombus.

Answer: **no**.

158.hint: Rectangle needs **four** 90° angles — check the list.
158.help:
Angles **95°** and **85°** are not right angles — **not** a rectangle.

Answer: **no**.

159.hint: Corresponding angles with parallel lines are equal.
159.help: Corresponding angles are **equal**: **70°** on the second parallel line.

160.hint: Two sides 7 cm and base 10 cm — equal pair?
160.help:
Two equal sides (7 cm and 7 cm) ⇒ **isosceles** triangle.

Example: a roof triangle with equal sloping edges.

161.hint: Square needs 90° angles — one angle 60° blocks that.
161.help:
Four equal sides with a **60°** angle is a **rhombus**, not a square.

Answer: **no**.

162.hint: Opposite sides of a door — parallel or perpendicular?
162.help: Top and bottom of a door are **parallel**; hinge side is perpendicular to top/bottom.

163.hint: Interior angle 120° — use regular polygon formula or trial.
163.help: Regular hexagons have **120°** interior angles — **6** sides.

164.hint: Polygons need straight segments — circle differs how?
164.help: Say **polygons** use **straight sides**; a **circle** is curved.

165.hint: Rhombus diagonals — right angle where they cross?
165.help:
Rhombus diagonals **meet at 90°**.

Answer: **true**.

Example: diagonals of a diamond shape cross at right angles.

166.help:
With a **90°** angle, the triangle is **right-angled** (even if other angles are acute).

Example: 35°, 55°, 90°.

167.hint: Two equilateral triangles glued on one full side — count outer edges.
167.help: The outline becomes a **rhombus** with **4** sides (two from each triangle exposed).

168.hint: One pair of parallel sides 8 cm and 5 cm.
168.help:
One parallel pair ⇒ **trapezium** family (UK name).

Example: a window with a slanted top edge.

169.hint: North to east is a quarter turn.
169.help:
A quarter turn from north to east is **90°**.

Example: turning right at a corner is often 90°.

170.help:
A **decagon** has **10** equal sides when it is **regular**.

Example: a ten-sided medal.

171.help:
Square diagonals **bisect at right angles**.

Answer: **always**.

172.hint: One pair parallel only — trapezium not parallelogram.
172.help: AB parallel to CD but AD not parallel to BC ⇒ **trapezium**.

173.hint: Three equal angles in a triangle — split 180° three ways.
173.help:
Each equal angle is **60°** because $180 ÷ 3 = 60$ in any triangle with three equal angles.

Example: equilateral triangle.

174.help:
Opposite sides of a parallelogram are **equal in length** — the statement is **false**.

Example: mark tick marks on opposite sides.

175.help:
$90 + 90 = 180°$ total turn for two right angles.

Example: turning left twice at square corners.

176.help:
Two equal angles (65° and 65°) ⇒ **isosceles** triangle; the third angle is 50°.

Example: a symmetric roof triangle.

177.help:
Every **rhombus** is a **parallelogram** — opposite sides **parallel**.

Answer: **yes**.

179.hint: Isosceles — mirror line through the equal sides?
179.help:
Non-equilateral **isosceles** triangles have **one** line of symmetry.

Answer: **true**.

180.hint: All sides 5 cm but angles 60° and 120° — all 90°?
180.help:
Not all angles are **90°** — **not** a square (it is a rhombus).

Answer: **no**.

181.help:
An **equilateral** triangle has **3** lines of symmetry — one through each vertex.

Example: fold the triangle in half three ways.

182.hint: Triangle angles must sum to 180° — check 90 + 95.
182.help:
$90 + 95 = 185 > 180$ — **impossible** in one triangle.

Example: angles in a triangle must always sum to 180°.

183.hint: Rectangle — opposite sides equal in pairs.
183.help: Rectangles **always** have **two pairs of equal opposite sides**.

184.hint: Regular octagon — sides and angles match.
184.help:
Say **eight equal sides** and **eight equal angles** on a regular octagon.

Example: a stop sign is a regular octagon.

185.help:
A **scalene** triangle usually has **no** line symmetry.

Answer: **false**.

186.hint: Three sides and three vertices — family name.
186.help:
Three sides and three vertices describe a **triangle**.

Example: a pizza slice outline is a triangle.

187.hint: Kite has four sides — quadrilateral family.
187.help:
A kite is always a **quadrilateral** (four sides).

Answer: **always**.

Example: a kite-shaped badge still has four sides.

188.hint: Rectangle always has four right angles — even with diagonal 13 cm.
188.help:
Any **rectangle** has **4** right angles, no matter how long its sides are.

Example: a door frame is roughly rectangular.

189.hint: All squares same shape — different sizes still similar.
189.help:
All **squares** are **similar** (same angles, sides in proportion).

Answer: **true**.

190.hint: From one vertex of a pentagon, diagonals to non-neighbour corners.
190.help: From one corner of a **pentagon**, you can draw **2** diagonals.

191.hint: Compare side counts: triangle vs quadrilateral.
191.help:
Triangles have **fewer** sides than quadrilaterals.

Answer: **never** more.

192.hint: Same size and shape — one word definition.
192.help:
**Congruent** means **same size and same shape** — one fits exactly on the other.

Example: two identical tiles.

193.help:
**200°** is a **reflex** angle and **greater than 180°**.

Answer: **true**.

194.hint: Parallelogram opposite angles equal — repeat 70° and 110°.
194.help:
The other two angles are **70°** and **110°** again (opposite angles equal in a parallelogram).

Example: 70°, 110°, 70°, 110° around the four corners.

195.help:
An **equilateral** triangle is a **regular** triangle.

Answer: **always**.

196.hint: Draw one pair of horizontal parallel sides and mark them.
196.help: Sketch a **trapezium**, mark the **parallel** pair with arrows.

197.hint: Use “equal” — four equal sides vs opposite sides equal.
197.help: Write that a **rhombus** has **four equal sides**, while a **parallelogram** only has **opposite sides equal**.

198.hint: Four right angles — square or just rectangle?
198.help:
Four right angles ⇒ **rectangle**; **square** only if sides also equal.

Answer: **sometimes**.

199.hint: Which vocabulary word means never meeting?
199.help:
**Parallel** lines never meet, even if extended, when they lie in the same plane.

Example: railway tracks viewed from far away.

200.hint: Pick real objects — state sides or angles for each.
200.help: Example: **book** (rectangle, **4** sides) and **sandwich cut** (triangle, **3** sides). Any sensible objects with correct properties.
